perturbation.original_text: “Information” means any and all confidential information or data exchanged, submitted or otherwise disclosed in respect of or further to the Purpose or prepared for or in relation to the Purpose, including but not limited to written proposal documentation, due diligence materials, contractual documentation, reports, and the fact that the Parties have entered into this Agreement and are discussing and considering a business relationship;
perturbation.changed_text: “Information” means any and all information, whether confidential or not, exchanged, submitted or otherwise disclosed in respect of or further to the Purpose or prepared for or in relation to the Purpose, including but not limited to written proposal documentation, due diligence materials, contractual documentation, reports, and the fact that the Parties have entered into this Agreement and are discussing and considering a business relationship;
perturbation.explanation: By removing the word 'confidential' from the definition of 'Information,' the scope of what is protected under the NDA is significantly broadened. This means even publicly available data would be subject to the agreement's confidentiality restrictions. This contradicts the fundamental purpose of NDAs, which is to protect genuinely confidential information.
perturbation.law_citation: UK Law: Saltman Engineering Co Ltd v Campbell Engineering Co Ltd [1948] 65 RPC 203
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the accepted legal understanding of what constitutes protectable information under a confidentiality agreement. The core principle is that the information must have the quality of confidence. By including non-confidential information, the agreement becomes potentially unenforceable because it seeks to protect information that is already in the public domain, undermining its fundamental purpose under contract law.
perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract text broadens the definition of 'Information' to include non-confidential data, which contradicts the fundamental purpose of Non-Disclosure Agreements (NDAs). The Law Explanation states that for information to be protectable under a confidentiality agreement, it must have the quality of confidence. By including non-confidential information, the agreement seeks to protect information already in the public domain. While there are no scraped law snippets to further reinforce this point, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation strongly support the conclusion that this change undermines the basic principles of contract law related to confidentiality agreements.

perturbation.original_text: take all necessary and proper security precautions (and at least as great as those it takes to safeguard its own information) to safeguard every part of the Information to prevent it from being disclosed or otherwise made available to any third party except as permitted by this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: take reasonable security precautions to safeguard every part of the Information to prevent it from being disclosed or otherwise made available to any third party except as permitted by this Agreement
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'all necessary and proper security precautions (and at least as great as those it takes to safeguard its own information)' to 'reasonable security precautions' significantly lowers the standard of care required. This creates ambiguity and weakens the protection afforded to the Disclosing Party's information. The original text sets a clear, objective standard by referencing the Receiving Party's own security measures; the modified text relies on a subjective assessment of 'reasonableness.'
perturbation.law_citation: UK Law: Blyth v Birmingham Waterworks Co [1856] 11 Exch 781
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification conflicts with the legal principles surrounding duty of care in contract law. While 'reasonable care' is a common standard, replacing a more specific, measurable standard with a more subjective one makes it harder to prove negligence or breach of contract. The original wording provided a benchmark (the Receiving Party's own security measures), which is absent in the modified version. This ambiguity could lead to disputes over whether the Receiving Party fulfilled its obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Blyth v Birmingham Waterworks Co - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Blyth v Birmingham Waterworks Company Court Exchequer Court Decided 6 February 1856 Citation (1856) 11 Ex Ch 781, 156 ER 1047 Transcript Full text of judgment Keywords Negligence, nuisance, reasonable foreseeability Blyth v Birmingham Waterworks Company (1856) 11 Ex Ch 781 [ 1 ] concerns reasonableness in the law of negligence . It is famous for its classic statement of what negligence is and the standard of care to be met. [ 2 ] Facts [ edit ] The defendants, Birmingham Waterworks Company , were the water works for Birmingham . They had been incorporated by statute for the purpose of supplying Birmingham with water. The statute provided that: the company should, upon the laying down of any main-pipe or other pipe in any street, fix, at the time of laying down such pipe, a proper and sufficient fire-plug in each such street, and should deliver the key or keys of such fire-plug to the persons having the care of the engine-house in or near to the said street, and cause another key to be hung up in the watch-house in or near to the said street. By sect. 87, pipes were to be eighteen inches beneath the surface of the soil. By the 89th section, the mains were at all times to be kept charged with water. The defendants derived no profit from the maintenance of the plugs distinct from the general profits of the whole business, but such maintenance was one of the conditions under which they were permitted to exercise the privileges given by the Act. The main-pipe opposite the house of the plaintiff was more than eighteen inches below the surface. The fire-plug was constructed according to the best known system, and the materials of it were at the time of the accident sound and in good order. The defendant had installed a fireplug into the hydrant near Mr Blyth's house. That winter, during a severe frost, the plug failed causing a flood and damage to Mr Blyth's house. Blyth sued the Waterworks for negligence. Judgment [ edit ] In establishing the basis of the case, Baron Alderson , made what has become a famous definition of negligence: Negligence is the om
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandated 'all necessary and proper security precautions (and at least as great as those it takes to safeguard its own information),' which sets a high and relatively objective standard. The changed text replaces this with 'reasonable security precautions,' a more subjective and potentially lower standard. This shift weakens the protection of the information. The law explanation highlights that the original wording provided a clear benchmark. The provided snippet from Blyth v Birmingham Waterworks Co discusses reasonableness in negligence, further emphasizing the legal significance of using 'reasonable care.' By moving from a defined benchmark to a reasonableness standard, the modified contract introduces ambiguity and lowers the duty of care, making it more difficult to prove negligence if a security breach occurs. This contradicts the intent of the original clause and weakens the legal obligation, increasing the risk for the disclosing party.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement and that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement and that the Disclosing Party may be entitled to specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'would not' to 'may not' and 'shall' to 'may' introduces uncertainty regarding the availability of specific performance and injunctive relief. Specific performance is an equitable remedy compelling the breaching party to fulfill the contract. Changing 'shall' to 'may' gives the court discretion, potentially undermining the Disclosing Party's ability to obtain this remedy, even in situations where monetary damages are inadequate.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Remedies for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_citation: UK Law: Beswick v Beswick [1968] AC 58
perturbation.law_explanation: This change creates a legal contradiction by weakening the availability of specific performance, a key equitable remedy. While courts have discretion in granting specific performance, the original clause indicated the parties' agreement that it was an appropriate remedy. The modification removes this assurance, potentially leading a court to deny specific performance even when it would be the most just outcome, thus contradicting the initial intent and commercially reasonable expectations of the parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Beswick v Beswick - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Beswick v Beswick The case concerned a widow who should have received a weekly annuity of five pounds from her nephew. Court House of Lords Decided 29 June 1967 Citation [1968] AC 58, [1967] UKHL 2 Transcript Full text of judgment Case history Prior action [1966] Ch 538, [1966] 3 WLR 396, [1966] 3 All ER 1 Court membership Judges sitting Lord Reid Lord Pearce Lord Upjohn Lord Guest Lord Hodson Case opinions Lord Reid, Lord Pearce, Lord Upjohn and Lord Guest Keywords Privity, third parties, consideration, specific performance Beswick v Beswick [1967] UKHL 2 , [1968] AC 58 was a landmark English contract law case on privity of contract and specific performance . The House of Lords , overruling the decision of Lord Denning in the Court of Appeal , ruled that a person who was not party to a contract had no independent standing to sue to enforce it, even if the contract was clearly intended for their benefit. Today the judicial precedent has been codified by statute in the United Kingdom, and Lord Denning's decision has largely been given effect by the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 . However the case remains good law in many other Commonwealth common law jurisdictions . Facts [ edit ] Lord Denning in the Court of Appeal started describing the facts of the case in the following way. Old Peter Beswick was a coal merchant in Eccles , Lancashire . He had no business premises. All he had was a lorry, scales, and weights. He used to take the lorry to the yard of the National Coal Board , where he bagged coal and took it round to his customers in the neighbourhood. His nephew , John Joseph Beswick, helped him in his business. In March 1962, old Peter Beswick and his wife were both over 70. He had his leg amputated and was not in good health. The nephew was anxious to get hold of the business before the old man died. So they went to a solicitor, Mr. Ashcroft, who drew up an agreement for them. The agreement was that Peter assign his business to his nephew in consideration of the nephew employing him for the rest of his life and then paying a weekly annuity to Mrs Beswick. Since the latter term was for the benefit of someone not party t
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates specific performance as a remedy for breach, while the changed text introduces uncertainty by stating specific performance 'may' be entitled. This weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to obtain this remedy, even when monetary damages are inadequate, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation states this change contradicts the initial intent and reasonable expectations. While the Wikipedia excerpt on Beswick v Beswick addresses privity of contract and specific performance, it does not directly bear on the contradiction created by weakening the 'shall' to 'may' obligation in the availability of specific performance in this case. However, the altered text's discretionary nature directly undermines the original agreement's intent, rendering the contract less enforceable and creating a legal contradiction as it deviates from the original clear obligation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement represents the entire agreement between the Parties in relation to the subject matter contained herein and supersedes all other agreements and representations, whether oral or written, between the Parties relating to such subject matter.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement represents the general agreement between the Parties in relation to the subject matter contained herein and complements all other agreements and representations, whether oral or written, between the Parties relating to such subject matter.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the 'entire agreement' clause (also known as an integration clause) to a 'general agreement' clause and states that it 'complements' other agreements rather than 'superseding' them. This fundamentally alters the legal effect of the clause. An entire agreement clause aims to limit contractual obligations to the terms within the written contract, preventing parties from relying on prior agreements or representations. The altered clause implies that other agreements and representations, even oral ones, may still be relevant, creating ambiguity and potential conflicts.
perturbation.law_citation: UK Law: Inntrepreneur Pub Co v East Crown Ltd [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 611
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts the purpose of an entire agreement clause. The 'Inntrepreneur Pub Co' case and related legal principles emphasize that entire agreement clauses are intended to provide certainty and limit liability by excluding reliance on pre-contractual statements. By stating that the agreement 'complements' other agreements, the modified clause undermines this objective and creates a risk of legal challenges based on external representations, going against established contract law principles.

perturbation.justification: The original text contains an 'entire agreement' clause, which is a legally recognized way to limit the scope of a contract to its written terms. The changed text replaces this with a 'general agreement' clause, which complements other agreements, explicitly contradicting the purpose of an entire agreement clause. According to the law explanation, the modified clause undermines the objective of providing certainty and limiting liability, and creates a risk of legal challenges. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by explaining the modification fundamentally alters the legal effect of the clause. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms that legislation.gov.uk carries most types of legislation and their accompanying explanatory documents; however, it does not provide specific details to justify the change. Thus the contradiction is based on the Law Explanation, the Perturbation Explanation, and the comparison of the original and changed text, with only general support from Scraped Law Snippet 1.
